title: Show HN: Homegames. An open-source game platform I've been making for 8 years
content:
The thread discusses the launch of **Homegames.io**, an open-source platform for browser-based games that the developer has been building for eight years.

### Key Points:
*   **Platform Architecture:** The games are primarily server-side, with thin clients rendering and sending inputs. The developer chose this approach to make multiplayer functionality easy to implement.
*   **User Feedback & Technical Issues:**
    *   **Traffic & Sessions:** The site experienced "too many requests" errors during the initial HN spike. Users questioned the necessity of server sessions for simple games, suggesting that single-player games should ideally run client-side to improve performance and accessibility.
    *   **Documentation:** Users requested better external documentation, noting that the "Studio" (editor) was behind a login screen. The developer agreed to provide better links and resources.
    *   **UI/UX:** Suggestions were made to refine the interface to better distinguish between joining multiplayer sessions and creating new ones.
*   **Community & Curation:**
    *   **"Weed Smoke Willie":** A debate arose regarding the appropriateness of "Weed Smoke Willie" being a featured game on the homepage. One user argued it might alienate potential users and negatively define the platform’s culture, while others defended it as harmless or a matter of creative freedom.
    *   **Inspiration:** Many users expressed nostalgia for 80s/90s game construction kits (like *Gorilla.BAS* or *Shoot 'Em Up Construction Kit*), praising the developer's dedication to bringing back simple, accessible browser gaming.
*   **Development Story:** The project evolved from a personal experiment in LAN gaming to a platform leveraging modern web speeds and AI-assisted coding, with the goal of capturing the spirit of classic web game portals like Newgrounds.

The developer acknowledged the feedback, expressing plans to optimize UI, improve documentation, and explore potential client-side/single-player modes for future iterations.

title: Show HN: KiCad in the Browser
content:
This thread discusses the launch of **PCBJam**, a project by Emergence Engineering that brings the KiCad PCB design suite to the web. The CTO, Viktor, shared details on the technical challenges of porting a complex C++ desktop application (which relies on OpenGL and wxWidgets) to WebAssembly and WebGL.

### **Key Technical Highlights**
*   **Performance:** The project achieves near-native performance by replacing KiCad's OpenGL graphics with custom WebGL code. 
*   **WASM Complexity:** Overcoming hurdles like pthreads in the browser, memory management, and bundle optimization (e.g., lazy-loading Open CASCADE) were central to development.
*   **Integration:** The team aims to keep their codebase close to the KiCad mainline to facilitate eventual merging.
*   **State:** It is currently in a "first-look" MVP phase with active development on browser compatibility and loading performance.

### **Community Feedback and Suggestions**
*   **Collaboration:** Users expressed excitement about the potential for real-time collaboration, educational use cases, and browser-based remote learning, which would lower the barrier for students who cannot install desktop software.
*   **Feature Requests:**
    *   **Integration:** Potential for PCB manufacturers (like JLCPCB/LCSC) to integrate design rules directly into the workflow.
    *   **Git Integration:** Users suggested native support for pushing/pulling projects from version control.
    *   **Plugin System:** Since standard Python plugins are difficult to run in-browser, the team plans to develop a new, web-friendly plugin or overlay API similar to Figma’s.
*   **Criticism:** Some users noted that the landing page appeared "AI-generated," which the creator acknowledged, promising to polish the messaging and copy for the official launch.

### **Future Roadmap**
*   **Business Model:** The team plans a "freemium" model, with a free tier and a ~$30/mo subscription for professional/closed projects, potentially adding AI-powered features later.
*   **UX/UI:** Immediate improvements will include better mobile support, progress bars for initial load, and "follow" features for collaborative sessions. 

The source code for the project is available on [GitHub](https://github.com/emergence-engineering/pcbjam).

title: Show HN: Osint tool that finds exposed files on domains
content:
The thread discusses the launch of **Cerast Intelligence**, an OSINT tool that monitors Certificate Transparency (CT) logs to identify newly exposed sensitive files (e.g., `.env`, `.git`, config files) on public domains.

**Key takeaways from the discussion include:**

*   **Functionality & Content:** The tool allows users to search for exposed files across the web. Participants noted a high frequency of leaked `.DS_Store` files and discussed the historical context of web misconfigurations, such as the early days of finding `/etc/passwd` via Google dorks.
*   **The Ethics of Automated Scanning:** A significant portion of the debate centers on the impact of mass-crawling. One user criticized the tool for "hammering" servers immediately upon certificate issuance, forcing developers to implement defensive measures like `fail2ban`. The developer countered by noting the inherent, persistent threat of malicious bots, while others debated whether "everyone does it" justifies the behavior.
*   **Operational Details:**
    *   The developer clarified that they crawl everything independently rather than scraping other services like LeakIX.
    *   To avoid potential legal or regulatory issues, the developer has explicitly blacklisted `.gov` domains.
*   **Observations on Data:** Users observed that many of the exposed sites appear to be low-security home projects or phishing sites rather than high-value enterprise infrastructure. 

The developer is actively seeking feedback on new features, such as notification alerts for specific keywords, and advice on mitigating abuse of the exposed data.
